    4. Your First "Social Experiments"
</h2>

  <!-- Attachment Blocks -->
<!-- DEBUG: attachments.count=4 -->

  <!-- DEBUG: attachment.id=45259782, kind=text -->

<div class="lecture-text-container">
<p></p><p><em>The reason you get all bent out of shape and moody about your art, music, photography, writing is because you’re taking everything too seriously. You think the challenges you face in your journey are like special snowflakes (they’re not). Your struggles are the same as what every one else out there (and throughout the history of art) has gone through. But the difference between experiencing it as a "healthy artistic struggle" or enduring it as a "dysfunctional artistic struggle" is in how you've decided to perceive your daily challenges. If you're upset all the time... I will prove to you that you’re looking at it wrong. In this module I’m going to show you a different way of looking at EVERYTHING you do so that your highs are higher (and more sustainable) and so that your lows... are always temporary (but still useful as fuck). </em></p><p></p>
</div>

<div class="lecture-text-container">
<p></p><h2 class="ql-align-center"><br></h2><h2 class="ql-align-center"><strong>"We find our voice through experimentation." - Rick Rubin</strong></h2><p><br></p><p>What if...</p><p>Instead of looking at your creative work as "making pretty things", or trying to get famous so you have a career, or clout chasing just to be seen, or trying to gain followers for validation...</p><p>What if you decided to look at things in a radical new way?</p><p><strong>A way that frees you from the game everyone else is playing.</strong></p><p>What if you started using your art-- and those apps on your phone that you use to share that art-- to conduct social experiments?</p><p><em>Social experiments that lead you to the things you're looking for faster.</em></p><p><br></p><h1><strong>1. Use Social Media To Conduct "Social Experiments".</strong></h1><p>Don't just use Instagram to post random shit ("post just to post"). Use Instagram to test ideas. Or to push the envelope. Or to see if you can attract a certain type of audience. Use it to see if you can influence "the normals" into paying more attention to the ideas you believe in. Or simply to see what you can get away with. Test the boundaries of society. Where can you move the borders artistically ? How can you mobilize the masses? What can you create that would make governments listen?</p><p>But the key is to approach it scientifically.</p><p>Write your 'hypothesis down'.</p><p>Try it out in the real world</p><p>Write the results.</p><p>Write down what you're gonna try next.</p><p>My buddy Trevor, created the first virtual influencer<a href="https://www.instagram.com/lilmiquela/" rel="noopener noreferrer" target="_blank"> Lil Miquela</a> with his partner Sara.</p><p>It was a social experiment.</p><p>Lil Miquela now has 3 million followers. She's been in W and Paper magazine. She's a sought-after model for luxury brands like Off White, Moncler, Prada, and Chanel.</p><p>What began as a "digital art experiment" has officially become a viral sensation.</p><p>Or how about Amalia Ulman?</p><p>Back in 2016, she created the first Instagram art experiment.</p><p><a href="https://www.elle.com/culture/art-design/a38857/amalia-ulman-instagram-artist/" rel="noopener noreferrer" target="_blank">Read about it here.</a></p><p>Or how about <a href="https://gritdaily.com/caroline-calloway-instagram-interview/" rel="noopener noreferrer" target="_blank">Caroline Calloway and her ongoing social experiment</a>. </p><p><br></p><h1><strong>2. Create Art Purely As An "Aesthetic Experiment".</strong></h1><p>Don't just take a "pretty photo".</p><p>Don't just release a "nice song". </p><p>Combine things that have never been combined. </p><p>See how fast you can create a valuable work of art.</p><p>Test how subversive you can be. Can you get whole new audiences to adopt your controversial ideas and help you spread them?</p><p>"Pretty" and "deep" aren't mutually exclusive. "Pretty" and "testing the boundaries of society" aren't mutually exclusive.</p><p>One word. Banksy.</p><p>Or at the other end of the spectrum.</p><p>WAP.</p><p>WAP is an étude in outrageousness. It is designed to get under your skin. Not just the song. But the video. The cameos. Everything. This song is one of the most successful social experiments of the year. </p><p>Madonna's whole career. Every single album she has ever released was an experiment.</p><p>The Weeknd's Trilogy was one of the most successful recent experiments in my memory: </p><p><em>"Can we create a rockstar and play Coachella without ever doing one interview or showing the face of an artist in todays selfie media saturated world?"</em></p><p>Beyonce's HUGE surprise album (no promo, no advance listens, totally contrary to the music industry) was a high stakes experiment. No major artist had done that before.</p><p>Imagine how, if you started treating your process as experiments... How much it would FREE you? </p><p>Oh, but you're more of a "brand" than an "artist"?</p><p>So-- Instead of just making consumer products, "test the market".</p><p>Push against the norms.</p><p>Just like Goop did when they released their <a href="https://shop.goop.com/shop/products/this-smells-like-my-vagina-candle?country=CAN" rel="noopener noreferrer" target="_blank">"This Smells Like My Vagina" candle</a>.</p><p>I mean... Corporations are taking more risks than you? The so-called "artist"?</p><p>That's tragic.</p><p><br></p><h1><strong>3. Treat Interviews As "Promotion Experiments".</strong></h1><p>Like Warhol did.</p><p>He treated all his interviews like they were art. They were so extraordinary they had to be compiled into <a href="https://www.amazon.com/Ill-Be-Your-Mirror-Interviews-ebook/dp/B009W73NW4/ref=sr_1_1?dchild=1&keywords=I%27ll+Be+Your+Mirror%3A+The+Selected+Interviews+by+Andy+Warhol&qid=1602849157&sr=8-1" rel="noopener noreferrer" target="_blank">a very thick book</a>: <a href="https://www.amazon.com/Ill-Be-Your-Mirror-Interviews-ebook/dp/B009W73NW4/ref=sr_1_1?dchild=1&keywords=I%27ll+Be+Your+Mirror%3A+The+Selected+Interviews+by+Andy+Warhol&qid=1602849157&sr=8-1" rel="noopener noreferrer" target="_blank">(I'll Be Your Mirror: The Selected Andy Warhol Interviews)</a></p><p>Warhol was so convinced that interviews should be treated like art that he started a magazine called 'Interview'.</p><p>Treat all your communication with the world as small social experiments.</p><p>Just like Jaden and Willow do.</p><p>'T Magazine' was their first social experiment to test the boundaries of their fame. Because they saw it that way in their mind, they gave themselves the license to go crazy.</p><p>They're still doing it.</p><p>What do you think the latest Willow interview about sexuality and blackness is all about?</p><p><br></p><h1><strong>4. Make YOURSELF "The Ultimate Experiment".</strong></h1><p>What would you be capable of if you put yourself under watch?</p><p>That's the question Benjamin Franklin (one of U.S.A.'s founding fathers and the guy who, you know... discovered electricity... no biggie) asked himself.</p><p>Benjamin Franklin used himself as "the ultimate experiment". And he chronicles it with incredible detail in "The Autobiography Of Benjamin Franklin".</p><p>His strategy was to master only one aspect of his personality at a time before he moved on to the next. He had 13 "virtues" that he wanted to achieve. And his progress was logged into a daily journal so he could make sure he was improving.</p><p>In his own words:</p><p><em style="color: rgb(41, 41, 41);">"My intention being to acquire all these good habits, I thought it would be well not to distract my attention by attempting the whole at once, but to fix it on one of them at a time; and, when I should be master of that, then to proceed to another, and so on, till I should have gone through all thirteen."</em></p><p><strong>Master one thing before progressing to the next.</strong></p><p>Everybody tries to change everything all at once. That’s why they fail. They also fail because they don't track their results. They think they're gonna remember. They're lying to themselves.</p><p>Oh, and how old was Benjamin Franklin when he came up with all of this stuff?</p><p>20 years old.</p><p><br></p><h1><strong>It's Not A Real Experiment Until You're Scientifically Tracking It</strong></h1><p>Don't be like the student who emailed me when I showed him the first draft of this module and said "I've BEEN doing social experiments, bro..." And when I asked him to show me his notes and documentation he said it's all in his head.</p><p>Yeah right. How did you feel on Sept. 17? Do you remember that? Nope. If you're not writing it down and 1. tracking your daily hypothesis and 2. tracking your results... Then you're just doing some random Shia Labeouf antics.</p><p>Expect those random results too.</p><p>No, if you want the benefits of what I’m talking about here get a notebook to do this. <strong>Be scientific and disciplined about it.</strong> That way you can do cool things like cross reference data ("Oh wow, every Tuesday 'THIS' happens...") That what will make it hella fun. I go as far as using graph paper to keep track of mine. Why graph paper? Purely aesthetic. It's fun. Sometimes I use Google spreadsheets ("What sub-messages get me the most engagement?").</p><p>This is where you will put <a href="https://learn.lastnightsparty.tv/courses/enroll/lectures/11925540" rel="noopener noreferrer" target="_blank" style="background-color: rgb(255, 255, 255);">The Mystery Formula </a>to good use.</p><p>Make entering BIG DATA part your daily ritual.</p><p>Test things and attach concrete numbers and word to the results.</p><p>For one ritualized moment at the end of your day... Turn everything you've observed in your experiment... into unemotional "data".</p><h3><strong><em>Be methodical so you don't ever have to be emotional. The emotions you possess should be reserved for the art.</em></strong></h3><p>The best part of this approach?</p><p><em>It will keep forcing you to look at the big picture. And you know why that’s important right? Because "zooming out" is what most artists suck at. They get lost in the details. They get lost in the trees </em><strong><em>and stop seeing the vibrant forest.</em></strong></p><p>But when you zoom out and do a BIG DAILY DATA DUMP, it helps you take things less seriously.</p><p><strong>Divorce yourself from being so emotional about social media, or the "art creation process", or about "other people's opinions".</strong></p><p>Now you can look at it all from a healthier lens.</p><p>Now everything you do online and offline will exist in service to a bigger reason.</p><p>Your little "secret project". </p><p>And this will result in less overthinking, less anxiety and less stress.</p><p>Other side effects you may experience? Your art starts evolving faster than ever.</p><p>Not a bad trade-off, huh? </p><p><span style="color: var(--squid-ink);">And the beauty is:</span></p><p>On the surface... You will LOOK like you're doing what everyone else is.</p><p>You will SEEM like you're blend in. </p><p><em>And that's why this is the ultimate Ninja move.</em></p><p>You APPEAR to be doing regular things... But onlookers will FEEL that something is different about your approach.</p><p><em>But they'll have no clue why.</em></p><p>"Dude, ever since you joined that Mystery School thing your IG has felt different. I don't get why. But I like it."</p><p>The "social experiments" approach is the difference between "sharing your sporadic freak-outs online" (what you were doing before) and "releasing a steady perfume of bottled enthusiasm" (what you'll be doing now).</p><p><span style="color: var(--squid-ink);">Suddenly the RIGHT people will be attracted to your energy. And the haters will have violent reactions to it... (just as you predicted).</span></p><p>You will be "trolling for 'the good'". </p><p>Now, if that sounds like too much thought and you prefer to release your art just for fun... Cool. </p><p>I'm not mad at that. Not at all. This lane is not for everyone.</p><p>But if you're career minded and you've been stressed out every time you think about the future...</p><p>And you constantly worry about whether or not people will ever notice what you're doing...</p><p>I invite you to change your relationship with your art -- and to the tools that you use to share and promote that amazing art.</p><p>Start playing a brand new game. A game you control.</p><p>Approach your career in a way so that you stay in "flow".</p><p><strong>Make your lows as useful as your highs.</strong></p><p>Like they say in silicone valley: "Fail fast, fail often".</p><p>Let people using "old school" ways fight each other for attention...</p><p>Rise above this obsolete kind of thinking-- And get an eagle's eye view of the land.</p><p></p>
</div>

    
  </div>
